speaker: interviewer
text: Melcer-- M-E-L-C-E-R on November 29, 1995, Deerfield Beach, Florida. Did you ever meet up with Walter C. [? Tobins? ?] I-- I don't know if that's the proper pronunciation.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, [? Tobins-- ?] [? Tobins ?] was the place my wife was working, and his factory was a couple blocks away from the building, on the outside of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your daughter during all of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter--

speaker: interviewer
text: The uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter-- as I said, she was-- before that, she wasn't working. She wasn't with my wife in the [? Tobins ?] factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did she leave the-- the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife stopped working in the-- well, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me? I mean, when did your daughter move out of the Warsaw ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when she went away to the Polish people? Eight days before the uprising. One-- a friend came in and said, I could save your kid, your girl, and we agreed to it, and the Polish people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a guy who was going-- going out and in the ghetto, bringing stuff and selling stuff, you know, taking out stuff from the ghetto and selling it on the Polish side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- no, I don't remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish guy, and he said, I think I can [INAUDIBLE] Polish people came. The women-- the Polish women came, and she took the girl, my baby, away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Jamiolkowsky is the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. J-A-M-I-O-L-K-O-W-S-K-Y, Jamiolkowsky. Janina Jamiolkowsky, her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you exchange money for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, without money. We just put together some clothing for the baby. She was 2 and 1/2 years old, and we gave her the clothing. That's all she got.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that the first civilian revolt in all of Europe took place in the Warsaw ghetto? Why was the first group of civilians to fight back-- why were they the Jews from Poland, from Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Warsaw had a lot of Jews. Half a million Jews lived in Warsaw, and there were a lot of organizations. And when they find out what's happening to the Jews in the camps-- not only camps, but just they're killing the Jews, so they see no end, no hope. This was the only way to do something about it, is it resist.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they able, these young men and women, able to resist general's troops, armies, and planes, and tanks, and panzers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had bought some ammunition, some revolvers or some-- whatever you could on the [INAUDIBLE] on the other side, on the Polish side. So they contacted the Polish side, and some stuff came in to some-- to be able to start the resistance. They thought maybe by starting a resistant, maybe the outside world would do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to protect yourself from the fires and the shooting that was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not protected at all. Could do nothing about it. The Germans came with heavy armory, and the result-- shooting on the buildings and put fires all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Can you describe your deportation from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I said, they were coming through the building, and everybody was called go down, and they took the people out to the railroads, and they send them away, and marching the people to the railroad, and they took them away by the trains. And we never heard of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what happened to you when they marched you away?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have-- as I said, through the building-- we couldn't go out of the building for the eight days. After the eight days, the Germans came, and they said, everybody down. And we came down, and they took us to the railroad. Very simple. And they took us to a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take you to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first camp was Poniatow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poniatow. P-O-N-I-A-T-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there by a couple weeks only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they took us out of this camp, to another camp, Dorohucha, D-O-R-O-H-U-C-H-A. Dorohucha. There, we were working on the [? turf, ?] cutting [? turf, ?] so using it as coal for the building. It was out and the winter comes, the wintertime, so you used this coal-- coal. And from Dorohucha, being there a couple months seems like the SS send a message to the Dorohucha Germans and all over to Poland and all other camps that the people who worked in Warsaw in the printing-- they have a list of 39 people-- that time-- no, that time was already 120 people in the printing-- on the end. And on this list were now our names, and they would call out these names in Dorohucha. And they took us out, me and my wife, and a couple more people were brought in this-- were in our group among the workers of this printing in Warsaw. And of this group, I don't remember how many there were, maybe eight or 10 people. They send us to Lublin. This was the place where of all camps, the people who worked and the printing supposed to come together. And then we all came together after-- it took another week or so. They took us-- the train-- to Radom, where the printing is, the new printing. All the people from other camps came together in this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let me ask you. Were you together with your wife still at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In all the camps and all the place, we were together till later on, when the Russians were marching-- marching to Lublin. They closed the printing in Radom. They took us on the way to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you describe your life in Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radom was a small place, and we had an apartment outside the printing. We went to morning to work, and evening we went to sleep in the apartment. There was no life in Radom, no community life. That's all to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you print in Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we printed all these stuff for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Police-- orders, you know, the-- not to shoot-- shoot so many people. You know. How's it called? [INAUDIBLE] publications. No, no, no. How do you call it? The bills-- billboards of the German. And for the officers, the German officers, the printing we needed and the envelopes and the shoes and all kinds of printing.

speaker: interviewer
text: In his book,

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Alexander Donat, D-O-N-A-T, stated, quote, 'What brought me back to life, pulled me out of the depths of despair, was the warmth, compassion, the kind words the Melcers gave me.' Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's quite a compliment. When did you meet Alexander Donat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know-- as a matter of fact, I met him at his printing. He was taken out from another camp. He was taken out from a Lublin camp. How is it called? Majdanek. He and his wife were in Majdanek. He was on the list. The wife was not. The wife was working in a pharmacy, so she was not on the list. But he came the same time with-- with us to Radom, and there we met. Was a linotyper, and I was compositor [INAUDIBLE] and other things. You know. So he was alone, and I was with my wife. For me, it was easier, everything. He was lost, so we took him in in our group, and we tried to do whatever we could for him. In [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he reported that Vladka Meed came to town, and that the Jews of Radom were frightened of her because they didn't know who she really was. And how did the Melcers fit into that scene? What did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so we-- we knew him from Warsaw as a neighbor-- her husband, but she came, and she had our names. She saw the girl. She saw our baby in New York in-- in Warsaw and she brought us money from the underground and documents for me and for my wife to try to get out-- I mean, to run away from the place, in case I have to.

speaker: interviewee
text: We took the money, and we divided to needy people. We know who-- who didn't have the money to buy extra food, you know what I mean? Because we were in Radom, but a lot of people from Radom still in the ghetto, and some of the people of Radom started working in the printing. [INAUDIBLE] were never printers. We have to cover and say that he's a printer, and we took him in. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, she came, and she brought us the documents and money, and we divided the money. We told everybody where the money comes from, and that's it. How was Vladka Meed able to travel around?

speaker: interviewee
text: She looked Polish, and she took a chance, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alexander Donat, in his book, reported that you were able to see your daughter, Alice, during this time. How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she arranged-- she told the people where we are. So the woman, Janina Jamiolkowsky, took the baby. As a matter of fact, she had her mother living the same city, Radom. But she went with the baby to Radom, and in the front of the building, she was-- my baby was standing near the window where I walk inside. And the-- but she and the baby was outside, and we saw the baby this way. She was walking back and forth on the street where the printing was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, nobody knew that she is visiting us, because we were afraid to tell anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Radom, where were you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Radom, where--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Radom printing shop break up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because as I said, the Russian army took Lublin  that time. It was not far away from Radom, was about 100 miles or something. So the printer closed, and we took everybody from the printing, and we on the march by foot to Auschwitz. On the way, a lot of people got killed, couldn't walk so much. And then, on the end, they put us on a train. We walked to Tomaszow, and in Tomaszow, we picked up a train to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, terrible conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way? Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very crowded. It was also a cattle train. And I don't know how we went-- there were a day or two took us to travel. No food, nothing until we came to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any of the local people, and did they see you during this journey to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean, we were-- we couldn't move. Everything-- wagon was closed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your arrival at Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we came to Auschwitz, some-- we had a couple deaths on the train. Some of the-- I think a friend of mine died in the same wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly, but he was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a good friend of ours, but he was a little, sickly man, and he died on the travel to-- travel to Auschwitz. And I remember we-- we came to Auschwitz, and they're saying, out! We went out, and we put in a line, line up everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then this guy, Mengele, was in front of us, and he did the selection, right and left. Whoever he did like, he put on this side, and we didn't know which is the right side. Some people tried to switch, you know what I mean? And it just happened that I was on the right side to wife-- I mean to transport, this was the line. And the next day, we were transported to Germany as part of the transport, I mean. Were you processed in-- in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I said. This was the process, left, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean, were you-- did you go through the showers and the removal of hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We went through the showers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- were-- did you receive a number at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That one day, no. The people who left, who stayed in Auschwitz, they get the-- got the numbers because we were on transport to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with when you arrived at the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you were separated, were you with anybody you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Donat was all the time with me, and my brother-in-law was all the time with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kotlar.

speaker: interviewer
text: His first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adas.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Adas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adas, A-D-A-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you separated from your wife, were you able to say anything to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, no, just-- she-- she went-- she went another line. The women were separated. They got out of the wagon, and we were separated. I couldn't see anything. I didn't know what happened to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone in the camp communicate with you? Any camp guard or inmate communicate to you what was about to happen or was happening there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were only one day in this camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, next day, we went another train and took us to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took us to Germany next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany-- where in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: The place-- the first place was Hessental. It's near Stuttgart. Everything's near south Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we worked there with-- cutting stones or something, that's all. We were there for a couple of weeks, and then they took us to Vaihingen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Vaihingen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaihingen. V-A-H-I-H-I-N-G-E-N, Vaihingen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the daily routine at Vaihingen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a working camp. I went every day to work in the morning, all kind of jobs, especially they brought-- the last time, we worked in the underground factory. They were building a underground factory because, you know, the airplanes used to come over. British airplanes came over all the time and bombarded the-- all around this place. And to end this, we were working in the factory at night. It was a night shift. During the night, British airplanes came in and bombarded the hole where the factory was supposed to be built, but the Germans run out. They took place in special-- the places safe-- for safety. And we were to stand out in the-- in the hole and watch that airplanes coming and bombarding the place. This was the worst place in camp, where-- this place. Then, we were transported. They took us out from this camp. This was when the American army start coming into Germany-- into Germany from the west. So we were transported to Dachau, Allach, Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city is Dachau near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau is near Munich. It's about 20 miles, I think, from Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dachau, we didn't work. This was-- Dachau was Allach, Dachau. [? Close to-- ?] Part of Dachau. We did not work. We didn't go out. We stayed all the time in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the daily routine there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no routine. We were all the time in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, what time did they get you up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in-- in the morning, you have to get up, appell, of course. And then, they didn't have no work for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was the food like in Dachau? What did they give you to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had in the mornings-- I don't remember exactly-- water, tea, sometimes something to it. And-- we didn't eat too much. We were all hungry, very hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions and the sleeping conditions like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks-- we sleep in the barrack, on the floor with-- with hay in it, whatever we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever offered a special privilege--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for extra work? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I never worked after this, really after Germany-- after the printing. I never did the right work. I have to work other things like everybody else. With stone, cutting stone, carrying this, carrying that. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many men lived in each barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple hundred.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was a barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the other inmates like? How did you get along with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have too many friends there. I mean, everybody was for himself. But there was nothing to do. All you have to do is sit in bed and doing nothing. Some groups went out to work. They took them out to work. I don't remember. They took us out to the work in-- in Allach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did some of--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about the end of the-- of the war. You know, we came to Allach probably in March, so we stayed a couple of weeks there. And at the end, when the-- the American army was near, they took out the people from Allach. And I don't know how it happened. They left a part of the people in Allach, and I was the part who stayed in Allach. The other part-- most people went on a march by foot to Garmisch-Partenkirchen, to the Alps by foot, and they were liberated there. On the way, some were killed too. And the place where I stayed, Allach, was bombarded by the American army. It took us two, three days. They bombed with a little fight with the Germans, probably. But we were standing in-- under the table, hiding in the barracks, so-- because of bombardment all around. And then the German-- the Americans came in. We saw no more Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was May 1. We're liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever sick when you were in any of these camps? Did you ever get ill?

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't get sick. If you're sick, you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a matter of fact, I was sick, and I had typhus. And Donat was in that camp. He was in Vaihingen. In Vaihingen, Donat was a sanitary. And he helped me when I had the typhus. I was taken into this place. It was not a hospital, just a separated-- a separate barrack of the sick people, and he helped me [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience kindness from a prison guard or a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any particular reasons why a person in a camp would be punished, or was it all random?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes it was random. Sometimes it was something-- they didn't like this. The German-- something didn't stay straight. They didn't do this, or they didn't do that. Some Germans like to punish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever punished?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was responsible for discipline among the inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- how is it called? Every group has a leader, and he was responsible. A kapo, they called.
